title: Unit 04 Pretest
description: Diagnostic pretest for Government Policy, Taxes, and Regulation.

questions.prompt: Why might government intervene in the economy?

choices.text: To provide public goods and respond when markets do not solve a problem well
choices.feedback: Correct. Governments may intervene to provide public goods and address market failures.

choices.text: To make every product free for consumers
choices.feedback: Government intervention does not mean making all goods free.

choices.text: To eliminate all business activity
choices.feedback: Government usually sets rules or provides services, not eliminate all business activity.

choices.text: To remove every choice from consumers
choices.feedback: Government intervention does not mean removing all consumer choice.

questions.prompt: A public good is something many people can use at the same time, such as streetlights.

choices.text: True
choices.feedback: Correct. Public goods are available for shared use and are often provided by government.

choices.text: False
choices.feedback: This is incorrect. Streetlights are a common example of a public good.

questions.prompt: Which type of tax is added when a person buys many goods or services?

choices.text: Income tax
choices.feedback: Income tax is collected from money earned, not directly from purchases.

choices.text: Property tax
choices.feedback: Property tax is based on the value of property.

choices.text: Sales tax
choices.feedback: Correct. Sales tax is added to many purchases of goods and services.

choices.text: Payroll tax
choices.feedback: That is not the best answer for a tax added to retail purchases.

questions.prompt: What is the main purpose of taxes in this unit?

choices.text: To raise revenue for government services and responsibilities
choices.feedback: Correct. Taxes help governments raise revenue to fund public goods, services, and operations.

choices.text: To guarantee that all businesses earn profits
choices.feedback: Taxes are not designed to guarantee profits.

choices.text: To stop consumers from buying goods
choices.feedback: Taxes may affect decisions, but their main purpose is revenue.

choices.text: To replace all other forms of government funding permanently
choices.feedback: This is too broad and does not describe the basic purpose taught in the unit.

questions.prompt: Expansionary fiscal policy may include increased government spending or lower taxes.

choices.text: True
choices.feedback: Correct. Expansionary fiscal policy is used to encourage economic activity.

choices.text: False
choices.feedback: This is incorrect. Expansionary policy often uses higher spending or lower taxes.

questions.prompt: Which action best represents contractionary fiscal policy?

choices.text: Increasing spending and cutting taxes
choices.feedback: That is closer to expansionary fiscal policy.

choices.text: Reducing spending or raising taxes to slow economic activity
choices.feedback: Correct. Contractionary fiscal policy is used to slow overall economic activity.

choices.text: Providing a public park for community use
choices.feedback: That example is more about public goods than fiscal policy strategy.

choices.text: Requiring labels on food products
choices.feedback: That is a regulation example, not contractionary fiscal policy.

questions.prompt: Why do governments use regulations in the economy?

choices.text: To protect consumers, workers, or the environment and guide business behavior
choices.feedback: Correct. Regulations are used to protect the public and shape how markets operate.

choices.text: To remove all competition from the market
choices.feedback: Regulation does not mean removing all competition.

choices.text: To prevent all businesses from making decisions
choices.feedback: Businesses still make decisions within regulated markets.

choices.text: To ensure that every product costs the same amount
choices.feedback: Regulations are not mainly about making all prices identical.

questions.prompt: Regulation can create public benefits, but it can also create economic tradeoffs.

choices.text: True
choices.feedback: Correct. Regulation often involves benefits for the public and costs or tradeoffs for businesses or markets.

choices.text: False
choices.feedback: This is incorrect. Regulations often involve both benefits and tradeoffs.
